name: Volume

sourceRefs.label: tnpscguru.in - 17-question real past-paper set on Volume
2025_q1 page 79 Q140 (sphere melted into a wire, with answer key — found while researching an earlier topic, stashed and used here)

researchNotes: This is the last of Part B Unit I's 10 named Aptitude skills. Strong web-sourced evidence (17 real past-paper questions found on one aggregator page) plus the previously-stashed sphere-melted-into-wire problem. Picked a representative spread across the recurring shapes: ratio-of-volumes-from-ratio-of-radii, melting/recasting (volume conservation), and surface-area-to-volume relationships — skipped near-duplicate ratio questions (multiple sourced items tested the exact same 'ratio of radii → ratio of volumes' pattern with different numbers).

reading.intro: Volume is the last of 10 named Aptitude sub-skills. Real exam evidence shows two dominant shapes: 'ratio of dimensions → ratio of volumes' (scaling), and 'melt/recast one solid into another shape' (volume is conserved, surface area is not) — both are core techniques worth knowing cold.

qa.question: Method — ratio of volumes from a ratio of radii (same shape): If the radius of one sphere is half that of another, what is the ratio of their volumes?
qa.answer: Volume scales with the CUBE of linear dimensions (V=4/3πr³), not directly with the radius. So if r₁:r₂=1:2, then V₁:V₂ = 1³:2³ = 1:8.

qa.question: Method — melting and recasting (volume is conserved, NOT surface area): A sphere of radius 9cm is melted and recast into a cone of the same radius (9cm). Find the cone's height.
qa.answer: Set sphere volume = cone volume: (4/3)π(9)³ = (1/3)π(9)²h → (4/3)(729) = (1/3)(81)h → 972 = 27h → h = 36 cm. The general shortcut: for melting sphere→cone of the SAME radius, height = 4×radius always (since the (4/3)r³ vs (1/3)r²h relationship reduces to h=4r when radii match).

qa.question: Real exam (already seen in an earlier topic, re-noted here): A sphere of diameter 8cm is melted and drawn into a wire of diameter 3mm. Find the wire's length.
qa.answer: Volume conserved: (4/3)π(4)³ = π(0.15)²×h → h ≈ 3792.6 cm ≈ 37.9 m. (Same melt/recast principle as r02, just sphere→cylinder/wire instead of sphere→cone.)

qa.question: Method — volume of a cylindrical tank from its capacity: A cylindrical tank has capacity 1,848 m³ and base diameter 14m. Find its depth.

Method — dug-out soil redistributed into a different shape (volume conserved): A cylindrical well of depth 20m, diameter 14m is dug; the soil is spread evenly to form a cuboid platform of base 20m×14m. Find the platform's height.
qa.answer: Well volume = (22/7)×7²×20 = 3,080 m³. Platform volume = 20×14×h = 280h. Set equal: 280h=3080 → h=11 m.

qa.question: Formula check: volume of a cube with side 5cm, and the relationship between a cube's volume and surface area.
qa.answer: Volume = side³ = 5³ = 125 cm³. If a cube's volume is 1000 cm³, its side = ∛1000 = 10cm, so its surface area = 6×side² = 6×100 = 600 cm² (cube surface area = 6×side², from its 6 square faces).

qa.question: Method — ratio of volumes for cone, sphere, cylinder of equal radius AND equal height: What is that ratio?
qa.answer: Cone : Sphere : Cylinder = 1 : 4 : 3, when all three share the same radius r and the same height h (for the sphere, 'height' = its diameter = 2r in this comparison). This is a frequently tested ratio worth memorizing directly.

questions.prompt: If the radius of sphere A is 3 times the radius of sphere B, what is the ratio of their volumes (A:B)?
A solid sphere of radius 6cm is melted and recast into a cone of the same radius. Find the cone's height.
questions.explanation: Using the shortcut h=4×radius for sphere→same-radius-cone: h=4×6=24cm.

questions.prompt: A cylindrical tank has capacity 4,620 m³ and base diameter 14m. Find its depth.
Find the volume of a cube with side 8cm.
If a cube's volume is 2,197 cm³, find its surface area.
A cone, a sphere, and a cylinder share the same radius and same height. What is the ratio of their volumes (cone:sphere:cylinder)?
questions.explanation: Cone:Sphere:Cylinder = 1:4:3 for equal radius and height.
